title: Thruth behind NGE EoE and Renewal of Evangelion
display_title: Thruth behind NGE EoE and Renewal of Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Thruth behind NGE, EoE and Renewal of Evangelion (?)             Quote Post by Seele_26 » Thu Oct 08, 2015 8:43 am Hi guys, i've checked out if someone alredy posted something about this, but i didn't find nothing so...Here it is. We of Evangelion Italian Fan have bought the Renewal of Evangelion [url]http://wiki.evageeks.org/Renewal_of_Evangelion[/url] because we were thinking it might contein something "new" about production information and stuff like that and actually we found something. I'm going to copy and paste the original article we wrote on our website (http://www.evaitalianfan.it/blog/nge-the-lost-numbers-english-version/), hoping someone could share his opinion about that. We asked Nipponbashi (italian cultural association about Japan culture) for a collaboration because, after buying the Renewal of Evangelion collector box , we watched and analyzed attentively its every single detail and frame for days and noticed some peculiar aspects, especially in its extras, where you can find videos made of working footage and sketches. The discoveries we made aren’tthe absolute truth, we cannot claim anything for certain as long as it isn’t officially confirmed by Khara; even so, we proceeded by trial and error and came to a single conclusion that could be shocking for most. The End of Evangelion wasn’t made in 1997. The End of Evangelion isn’t the end Anno wanted. The End of Evangelion (or the episodes Air and Mogokoro wo Kimi Ni) wasn’t made to be a sequel film released after the anime, but to be the concluding episodes right after The World Ending/Do you love me?andThe Beast that Shouted Love at the Heart of the World/Take Care of Yourself. It is well-known that many scenes and frames of Evangelion, especially in the TV series, were re-used many times because of budget cuts; nonetheless, we noticed some differences between the information we got from the Renewal’s The End of Evangelion working sketches and the information released elsewhere. You will find the pictures (with related description) that made us develop our theory here below. Pictures taken from the episode 25 preview at the end of episode 24 (Italian Platinum Edition) SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Oct 08, 2015 11:41 am This is mostly old news. The sketchy EoE previews were added to the DC versions of the episodes and were not in the original version. Since the DC eps were made after EoE this isn't a shocking development, it's an attempted retcon. That said, I appreciate the effort and attention to detail.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Oct 08, 2015 2:25 pm The animatics included on the Renewal box-set extras showing a TV format version of part of episode 25 like are an interesting part of the cutting-room floor "what-if"s that went on with the series, ever since the proposal.

[]:             Quote Post by TMBounty_Hunter » Thu Oct 08, 2015 3:57 pm That still shows the framing lines for the intended wide theatrical aspect ratio. It's only 4:3 because that's what the animation testing video equipment was limited to.

I'm going to copy and paste the original article we wrote on our website (http://www.evaitalianfan.it/blog/nge-the-lost-numbers-english-version/), hoping someone could share his opinion about that. We asked Nipponbashi (italian cultural association about Japan culture) for a collaboration because, after buying the Renewal of Evangelion collector box , we watched and analyzed attentively its every single detail and frame for days and noticed some peculiar aspects, especially in its extras, where you can find videos made of working footage and sketches. The discoveries we made aren’tthe absolute truth, we cannot claim anything for certain as long as it isn’t officially confirmed by Khara; even so, we proceeded by trial and error and came to a single conclusion that could be shocking for most. The End of Evangelion wasn’t made in 1997. The End of Evangelion isn’t the end Anno wanted. The End of Evangelion (or the episodes Air and Mogokoro wo Kimi Ni) wasn’t made to be a sequel film released after the anime, but to be the concluding episodes right after The World Ending/Do you love me?andThe Beast that Shouted Love at the Heart of the World/Take Care of Yourself. It is well-known that many scenes and frames of Evangelion, especially in the TV series, were re-used many times because of budget cuts; nonetheless, we noticed some differences between the information we got from the Renewal’s The End of Evangelion working sketches and the information released elsewhere.
